# Deployment Guide {#deployment}

## Running Locally for Development

The fastest way to get the glycoMusubi Shiny app running is on your own
machine. Install the required R packages and launch the app on port
3838:

```r
install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib", "DT", "plotly", "visNetwork",
                    "arrow", "data.table", "shinycssloaders", "shinyWidgets"))
shiny::runApp("shiny_app/", port = 3838)
```

Before launching, you need to tell `global.R` where the data lives by
setting three environment variables. `GLYCOKG_KG_DIR` points to the
knowledge graph parquet and edge files (defaulting to `../data/kg` if
unset), `GLYCOKG_DATA_DIR` points to the processed annotation tables
(defaulting to `../data/processed`), and `GLYCOKG_EXP_DIR` points to
experiment results and embeddings (defaulting to
`../data/experiments`). Export them in your shell session before
starting the app:

```bash
export GLYCOKG_KG_DIR=/path/to/kg_data
export GLYCOKG_DATA_DIR=/path/to/processed_data
export GLYCOKG_EXP_DIR=/path/to/experiments
```

Inside `global.R`, these are read with fallback defaults so the app
can start even without explicit configuration:

```r
kg_dir   <- Sys.getenv("GLYCOKG_KG_DIR",  unset = "../data/kg")
data_dir <- Sys.getenv("GLYCOKG_DATA_DIR", unset = "../data/processed")
exp_dir  <- Sys.getenv("GLYCOKG_EXP_DIR",  unset = "../data/experiments")
```

## Deploying with Docker

For reproducible, isolated deployment, Docker is the recommended
approach. The Dockerfile starts from the `rocker/shiny-verse:4.4.1`
base image, installs system dependencies and the required R packages,
copies the app code into the Shiny Server directory, and exposes port
3838:

```dockerfile
FROM rocker/shiny-verse:4.4.1
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev libxml2-dev libglpk-dev cmake \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
RUN install2.r --error --skipinstalled \
    bslib DT plotly visNetwork arrow data.table shinycssloaders shinyWidgets
COPY shiny_app/ /srv/shiny-server/glycokg/
EXPOSE 3838
CMD ["/usr/bin/shiny-server"]
```

The accompanying `docker-compose.yml` wires up the data volumes as
read-only mounts, passes the environment variables, limits memory to
4 GB, and configures automatic restarts:

```yaml
version: "3.9"
services:
  glycokg-app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "3838:3838"
    volumes:
      - ./data/kg:/srv/data/kg:ro
      - ./data/processed:/srv/data/processed:ro
      - ./data/experiments:/srv/data/experiments:ro
    environment:
      - GLYCOKG_KG_DIR=/srv/data/kg
      - GLYCOKG_DATA_DIR=/srv/data/processed
      - GLYCOKG_EXP_DIR=/srv/data/experiments
    restart: unless-stopped
    mem_limit: 4g
```

Building and running is straightforward. Use `docker compose build`
to create the image (add `--no-cache` for a clean rebuild),
`docker compose up -d` to start in detached mode, `docker compose
down` to stop, and `docker compose logs -f` to stream live logs.

```bash
docker compose build
docker compose up -d
docker compose logs -f
```

## Deploying to shinyapps.io

The shinyapps.io free tier imposes a 1 GB storage limit, and the full
dataset with embeddings weighs roughly 12 GB, so a reduced bundle is
necessary. The parquet-only dataset (roughly 250 MB) fits comfortably.
Deploy with a single command:

```r
rsconnect::deployApp(appDir = "shiny_app/", appName = "glycoMusubi")
```

To slim down the bundle, ship only parquet files, subset experiments
to the most important configurations, and create an
`.rsconnectignore` file to exclude large directories:

```text
data/experiments/embeddings/
data/raw/
*.rds
```

## University Server Deployment

University environments typically require user isolation and
institutional authentication. ShinyProxy addresses both by launching a
fresh Docker container per user session. The `application.yml`
configuration specifies the container image, data volume mounts, and
LDAP authentication against the university directory:

```yaml
proxy:
  title: glycoMusubi
  port: 8080
  authentication: ldap
  ldap:
    url: ldap://ldap.example.edu:389/dc=example,dc=edu
    user-search-filter: (uid={0})
  specs:
    - id: glycokg
      container-image: glycokg-app:latest
      container-volumes:
        - /data/glycokg/kg:/srv/data/kg:ro
        - /data/glycokg/processed:/srv/data/processed:ro
```

For HTTPS, place an Nginx reverse proxy in front of ShinyProxy with a
Let's Encrypt certificate. The key configuration details are WebSocket
upgrade support (required by Shiny) and a generous read timeout to
accommodate long-running computations:

```nginx
server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    server_name glycokg.example.edu;
    ssl_certificate     /etc/letsencrypt/live/glycokg.example.edu/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/glycokg.example.edu/privkey.pem;
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8080;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
        proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
        proxy_read_timeout 600s;
    }
}
```

Generate the certificate with
`sudo certbot --nginx -d glycokg.example.edu`. If ShinyProxy's LDAP
integration is not available, the `shinymanager` package adds a login
page directly to the app with
`ui <- shinymanager::secure_app(ui, enable_admin = TRUE)`.

## Performance Tuning

Three areas deserve attention when scaling the app. For memory
management, use Arrow's lazy loading to avoid reading entire parquet
files into memory, and rely on `data.table` for large in-memory
joins:

```r
nodes <- arrow::open_dataset(file.path(kg_dir, "nodes.parquet"))
edges <- arrow::open_dataset(file.path(kg_dir, "edges.parquet"))
```

For concurrency, remember that a single R process handles one user at
a time. You can scale horizontally with ShinyProxy (one container per
user), Shiny Server Pro workers, or
`docker compose up --scale glycokg-app=3` behind a load balancer.

For caching, wrap expensive reactives with `bindCache()` so repeated
queries with identical inputs are served from cache rather than
recomputed:

```r
filtered_data <- reactive({
  nodes |> dplyr::filter(type == input$node_type) |> dplyr::collect()
}) |> bindCache(input$node_type)
```

## Monitoring the Running App

Keep an eye on the app in production using Docker's built-in tooling.
`docker stats glycokg-app` shows live CPU and memory usage,
`docker compose logs -f --tail=100` streams the most recent
application logs, and you can inspect Shiny Server's own logs with
`docker exec -it glycokg-app tail -f /var/log/shiny-server/*.log`.
Watch for "Connection closed" warnings, which often signal
insufficient memory, and slow render times in the log output, which
suggest a need for caching or data subsetting.
